posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
Looks like '14 Cristal was released in the UK yesterday and the best price I saw was $270 US at current FX. I was told it was going to be released in the US in March or April and IIRC the US release price is typically about $50 - $70 more than UK. Flickinger is offering it pre-arrival for $335. That is about $100 increase over the first US pricing I saw for '08 when it was released, and '13 is still available in the states for $260/bottle. Falstaff rated it 100pts and The Buyer.net said it did not reach the symphonic heights of the '13. Unfortunately, no WK review - yet. Jean-Baptiste Lécaillon described it as follows:

“It is showing a lot, 2014 is a very expressive wine – very complex fruit, flavours and richness of aromatics – but it has more freshness than 2012, more precise definition, more pixels in 14 than 12. It is a beautiful vintage and I have not seen this beauty when so young, I didn’t imagine it would show this so early from release.”

If I am reading between the lines correctly here, I believe he is saying it is better than '12, and I rank '12 behind '08 and '13 so maybe this fits between '13 and '12. Seems like the best trade at the moment would be to buy more '13 and wait and see if '14 sells at the current price. If the vintage charts are any indication, '15 should be a stellar wine and maybe priced accordingly.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
The Vinous review is supposed to be published on Thursday.

Which is interesting since the importer already has Galloni’s review of the '14 up on its website. Subscribers don’t have this yet?

Vinous Media
98 Points
January 2022, Antonio Galloni

“The 2014 Cristal is another in a long line of gorgeous wines from Roederer and long-time Chef de Caves Jean-Baptiste Lecaillon. A dazzling, vibrant Champagne, the 2014 impresses with a stunning combination of fruit density and linear energy that bring to mind a hypothetic blend of 2012 and 2008. At times, the bubbles feel totally wrapped up in waves of creamy, resonant fruit to the point the mousse is barely perceptible, especially with aeration. The flavors are bright and finely sculpted, leaning very much into the citrus, floral and mineral end of the spectrum. The blend is a classic: 60% Pinot Noir and 40% Chardonnay taken from 39 of the 45 parcels that comprise the Cristal estate. In most vintages, the Pinot components are roughly equally split between Verzy/Verzenay and Ay, but in 2014, Lecaillon favored Verzy and Verzenay, two north facing villages in the Montagne de Reims (where rain was less problematic) known for conferring saline energy and vibrancy. Rain was much more of an issue in the Vallée de la Marne. Consequently, some of the Pinots in Ay were diluted and did not make it into the blend. Chardonnays were sourced from historic plots in Avize, Mesnil and Cramant.”

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I mean I guess the advantage Roederer has is a huge selection of vineyards so they may be a little less susceptible to vintage variation compared to other producers.

There are plenty of big houses who can draw on a broad palette of crus. The advantage Roederer has over many others is that they have invested seriously in farming better for over two decades now. The best 2014 vintage Champagnes were produced by good farmers. Of course, that’s true every year, but a challenging vintage throws it into relief.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
I mean I guess the advantage Roederer has is a huge selection of vineyards so they may be a little less susceptible to vintage variation compared to other producers.

There are plenty of big houses who can draw on a broad palette of crus. The advantage Roederer has over many others is that they have invested seriously in farming better for over two decades now. The best 2014 vintage Champagnes were produced by good farmers. Of course, that’s true every year, but a challenging vintage throws it into relief.

It isn’t just a wide selection of vineyards, ownership of these vineyards, or better farming, but also the location of the vineyards. While it paints a broad stroke, there is a reason that villages are Grand Cru and Premier Cru (especially high ranking Premier Cru on the old scale). In the more challenging or worst vintages, the top villages really stand out - especially the top locations in these top villages. If you have a good mix of estate owned land in prime locations in the top villages across both sides of the Montagne, the Cote des Blancs, and the Grande Valle you are going to do well in years like 2014 which are average in quality and classically known as a ‘NV material’ year. When you throw in proper farming and advanced, forward thinking winemaking like Roederer then you really take things to another level.

Slight tangent - The estate owned/farmed vines that go into DP (which is a large majority) isn’t at quite the same level as Roederer in terms of farming, but it is not all that bad. There is a reason that Cristal and DP can be made in such high quality and quantity (relatively speaking Cristal is a high production wine even if it is dwarfed by DP). Even before the focus on improving the farming and winemaking both DP and Cristal were very, very good wines. Are they better now and with even higher production? Yes, but the core is still that they have lots of estate owned land in the best locations in the best villages. Just imagine what JB at Roederer could do in terms of quality and quantity if he had DP’s land (and a lot more workers)…

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked: 60% Pinot Noir and 40% Chardonnay is the approximate typical blend you will see on Cristal. It varies a bit per the vintage. 32% seems to be the most recent typical amount of oak use. My notes for the 2014 say approximately 60% Pinot Noir and 40% Chardonnay with 27% oak. To me, this means that the blend is likely something other than the average 60% Pinot and 40% Chardonnay, but the oak is probably 27%. I expect neither 60% Pinot Noir/40% Chardonnay nor 32% oak to be exactly correct for the 2014 despite what the website says.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I mean I guess the advantage Roederer has is a huge selection of vineyards so they may be a little less susceptible to vintage variation compared to other producers.

There are plenty of big houses who can draw on a broad palette of crus. The advantage Roederer has over many others is that they have invested seriously in farming better for over two decades now. The best 2014 vintage Champagnes were produced by good farmers. Of course, that’s true every year, but a challenging vintage throws it into relief.

It isn’t just a wide selection of vineyards, ownership of these vineyards, or better farming, but also the location of the vineyards. While it paints a broad stroke, there is a reason that villages are Grand Cru and Premier Cru (especially high ranking Premier Cru on the old scale). In the more challenging or worst vintages, the top villages really stand out - especially the top locations in these top villages. If you have a good mix of estate owned land in prime locations in the top villages across both sides of the Montagne, the Cote des Blancs, and the Grande Valle you are going to do well in years like 2014 which are average in quality and classically known as a ‘NV material’ year. When you throw in proper farming and advanced, forward thinking winemaking like Roederer then you really take things to another level.

Slight tangent - The estate owned/farmed vines that go into DP (which is a large majority) isn’t at quite the same level as Roederer in terms of farming, but it is not all that bad. There is a reason that Cristal and DP can be made in such high quality and quantity (relatively speaking Cristal is a high production wine even if it is dwarfed by DP). Even before the focus on improving the farming and winemaking both DP and Cristal were very, very good wines. Are they better now and with even higher production? Yes, but the core is still that they have lots of estate owned land in the best locations in the best villages. Just imagine what JB at Roederer could do in terms of quality and quantity if he had DP’s land (and a lot more workers)…

Well, that is one of those chicken-and-egg sort of debates that one can get into, and nor would I want to minimize the importance of optimally sited and composed vineyards; but when you look at what a grower such as Olivier Collin did in 2014, working with holdings in Congy, classified at 85%, and also subject to the very high rainfall that also impacted the Côte de Blancs, then I think the case can be made that farming is just as decisive.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I don’t get the first sentence: according to the Roederer website, it’s the same percentage of pinot as the last four vintages (60%).

It seems you don’t subscribe to RP. WK presents a larger percentage of Pinot.

I do subscribe. I suppose Roederer could be on autopilot regarding info on their website, but I usually go by the producer’s info. Everyone else is also going with 60/40 split on variety.

I wonder if WK got the chardonnay percentage mixed up with the oak percentage (32%).

No, I didn’t - 32% was what I heard JBL to say over the phone yesterday morning (he was at a railway station and I had a one year old shouting in the background, but I do think I heard him correctly). He added that 68:32 is the ratio of Pinot to Chardonnay in the parcels that can go into Cristal. If I misheard I’ll amend.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
William,

I agree with everything you have said. My point was that when you have the advantage of the best plots in the best villages then the best farming (and winemaking) is going to lead to better results (most of the time, but not always) than someone with the best farming and winemaking, but slightly lesser plot locations. Roederer has maximized what they have and this happens to be maximizing some of the best plots in Champagne. I’m not trying to take away from anyone else, but when you have the best of the best and then you maximize it… you get the recent run of Cristal vintages. I am in awe of what Jean-Baptiste and Roederer have done. Most larger producers never really maximize or exceed what they ‘could’ do.

Collection 242 is worth your time - it’s a really fantastic NV, especially with the percentage of reserve wines that go into it.

I have had this a few times and have been underwhelmed each time.

I am sure 14 Cristal will be a great wine and I’m sure I’ll still get some (if not as much as prior vintages, unless the price comes down), but I don’t find the 242 to be even close to as good. It’s not awful, but just unremarkable.

It’s not as good because it’s not $300, it’s $60. For those of us who don’t play in the Cristal realm, it represents a real access point to the work Roederer has been doing. I think 242 is a great wine and a great Champagne with few peers in its price range.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It seems you don’t subscribe to RP. WK presents a larger percentage of Pinot.

I do subscribe. I suppose Roederer could be on autopilot regarding info on their website, but I usually go by the producer’s info. Everyone else is also going with 60/40 split on variety.

I wonder if WK got the chardonnay percentage mixed up with the oak percentage (32%).

No, I didn’t - 32% was what I heard JBL to say over the phone yesterday morning (he was at a railway station and I had a one year old shouting in the background, but I do think I heard him correctly). He added that 68:32 is the ratio of Pinot to Chardonnay in the parcels that can go into Cristal. If I misheard I’ll amend.

Update: JBL says it is 60:40, so evidently I did mishear. The site will be amended when the relevant people are awake.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked: I’m not exactly sure, why the Cristal costs that much in the US. Yes, the price is higher than in previous vintages, but I see offers in France, Germany, Switzerland, UK around 250 US Dollars (with taxes already included). Was there always such a huge price difference between the US and Europe? Anyway, the price here in Europe is at a level you can still buy the 2008 and about 30 Dollars above the 2012 and 2013.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
I’m not exactly sure, why the Cristal costs that much in the US. Yes, the price is higher than in previous vintages, but I see offers in France, Germany, Switzerland, UK around 250 US Dollars (with taxes already included). Was there always such a huge price difference between the US and Europe? Anyway, the price here in Europe is at a level you can still buy the 2008 and about 30 Dollars above the 2012 and 2013.

For the vintages you listed, the difference in release price between the UK and US has been about $50. The best price I can see right now in the UK in WS Pro for '08 Cristal is just under $400/btl from BI and that is guaranteed stock. In GBP, that is a double from early '20. The '13 is still available in the US, my LWS has it for $259 and I can get cases. That is basically the same price as the UK. Interesting (or maybe sad) to note that there was a time when '08/'12/'13 were trading in the UK at the equivalent of around $185 US given the then prevailing FX rates. The '08 was in '19 and the '12/'13 were in '21.

The demand for Champagne in the UK market has jumped dramatically in the past 6 months. Not sure if that demand is being driven by speculators or Champagne drinkers. There has also been a lack of supply. '12 DP is significantly cheaper in the US now than the UK ($180 from my LWS vs $215 at BI) and the release price in the UK was around $150.

posts.name: Matt Porter
posts.cooked:
Looks like '14 Cristal was released in the UK yesterday and the best price I saw was $270 US at current FX. I was told it was going to be released in the US in March or April and IIRC the US release price is typically about $50 - $70 more than UK. Flickinger is offering it pre-arrival for $335. That is about $100 increase over the first US pricing I saw for '08 when it was released, and '13 is still available in the states for $260/bottle. Falstaff rated it 100pts and The Buyer.net said it did not reach the symphonic heights of the '13. Unfortunately, no WK review - yet. Jean-Baptiste Lécaillon described it as follows:

“It is showing a lot, 2014 is a very expressive wine – very complex fruit, flavours and richness of aromatics – but it has more freshness than 2012, more precise definition, more pixels in 14 than 12. It is a beautiful vintage and I have not seen this beauty when so young, I didn’t imagine it would show this so early from release.”

If I am reading between the lines correctly here, I believe he is saying it is better than '12, and I rank '12 behind '08 and '13 so maybe this fits between '13 and '12. Seems like the best trade at the moment would be to buy more '13 and wait and see if '14 sells at the current price. If the vintage charts are any indication, '15 should be a stellar wine and maybe priced accordingly.

A little surprised to not see a review in the champ exclusive WK put out yesterday.

Proof-reading and editing mean that there is a ten day lag, in general, between me finishing the notes and them getting published! And the 2014 Cristal only arrived on Monday. But you will see something out very soon

Look forward to reading your take!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
I bought more '13 this morning. Early days yet, but it looks like an equal of '08.

Here were my thoughts from a month or so ago.

2013 Louis Roederer Cristal

Bit of lovely white peach, lemon zest and brioche on the nose.

Zingy acidity and slate on the palate with some more lemon and kaffir lime. Great focus and as others have said, a bit more generosity than I expected.

Super long finish. Beautiful.

For me, right now I’d rate the recent Cristals 08 > 09 > 13 slightly > 12 atm, but they’re all very close.
